variables.content: For discrete DMC single-particle band data E(k_i) in a periodic finite simulation cell, performing a quartic fit over a moderately wide wave-number interval k∈[k_F−Δk,k_F+Δk]—meaning Δk large enough to include multiple quantized k points on both sides of k_F rather than being restricted to an infinitesimal neighborhood of k_F—reduces sensitivity to localized anomalous fluctuations or outlier derivative estimates in the immediate vicinity of k_F and produces per-size effective-mass estimates that are more stable with respect to statistical noise and more amenable to controlled finite-size extrapolation.

To obtain the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass from finite-N DMC band data one may (i) fit the computed isotropic band values E(k), with k = |𝗸|, in a finite interval k∈[k_F−Δk,k_F+Δk] about the Fermi wave vector k_F to the quartic polynomial E(k)=α_0+α_2 k^2+α_4 k^4 (with fit coefficients α_0, α_2, α_4), (ii) compute the band derivative at k_F from the fit as (dE/dk)_{k_F}=2α_2 k_F+4α_4 k_F^3 and define the quasiparticle effective mass by m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F}, and (iii) evaluate m^*(N) for several simulation-cell electron counts N and fit the N dependence to the single power-law model m^*(N)=m^*(∞)+b N^{-γ} (with fit parameters m^*(∞), b, and γ) to obtain the thermodynamic-limit mass m^*(∞); when the quartic fit in step (i) is performed over a sufficiently wide Δk about k_F (so as not to be dominated by pathological near-k_F fluctuations) this three-step procedure yields stable extrapolated values m^*(∞).

Systematic finite-size biases in the DMC effective mass m* can be eliminated by the following protocol: compute DMC total-energy differences to obtain quasiparticle energies at multiple momenta k in the interval 0 ≤ k < 2 k_F for a sequence of finite periodic simulation-cell electron numbers N (paramagnetic N up to 274 electrons were used); fit the computed interacting band values E(k) at the sampled k points to a smooth rational (Padé) function of the form E_fit(k) = (A_0 + A_1 k + A_2 k^2 + A_3^2 k^3)/(1 + 2 A_3^2 k), where A_0, A_1, A_2, and A_3 are real fit parameters determined by least-squares fitting; extract the finite-N effective mass m*(N) = k_F / (dE_fit/dk)_{k_F} from the fitted band; and extrapolate m*(N) linearly as a function of 1/N to N → ∞ to obtain the thermodynamic-limit m*. Cross-checks performed with quartic polynomial fits to E(k) show that the choice between the Padé form and quartic polynomials has a negligible effect on the extrapolated m* values for the densities and spin polarizations studied.

Given discrete DMC band points that suffer from local statistical noise and from Hartree-Fock-like pathological behavior in the vicinity of $k_F$ (large, fluctuating numerical derivatives due to incomplete retrieval of correlation energy), the protocol of fitting a global quartic $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_0+\alpha_2 k^2+\alpha_4 k^4$ to occupied-band points over a wide $k$ range and using its derivative at $k_F$ to estimate $m^{*}$ is justified by the assumptions that (a) this global fit suppresses local pathological fluctuations that would otherwise dominate numerical derivatives evaluated only near $k_F$, and (b) the global fit does not introduce a systematic bias from including data far from $k_F$ that would alter the fitted slope at $k_F$ in a way unrepresentative of the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle derivative.

Numerical derivatives of finite-N DMC energy bands are unstable at k≈k_F: the derivative (dE/dk) evaluated from discrete finite-N DMC data often exhibits outlier points in the immediate vicinity of the Fermi surface, a pathology attributable to the combination of (i) the divergence or pathological behavior of the Hartree-Fock band derivative at k_F and (ii) DMC in a finite cell retrieving only a finite fraction of the exact correlation energy when using a fixed-node Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave function; consequently, robust estimates of the quasiparticle effective mass are obtained by fitting the band over a finite wave-number range Δk about k_F (using, for example, the quartic parametrization E(k)=α_0+α_2 k^2+α_4 k^4) and using the fit-derived derivative at k_F to average over and suppress the near-k_F anomalies.

Given a discrete set of DMC-computed occupied-band samples $\{\mathcal{E}_i\}$ at wave-vector magnitudes $\{k_i\}$ with $0\le k_i\le k_F$, the proposition is that performing a least-squares fit of a smooth quartic function $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_0+\alpha_2 k^2+\alpha_4 k^4$ over the full occupied range produces an estimate of the derivative $\partial\mathcal{E}/\partial k$ at $k=k_F$ that is substantially less sensitive to local numerical noise, Hartree-Fock-like residual pathological fluctuations near $k_F$, and finite-size irregularities than a numerical derivative obtained by differencing only a few near-$k_F$ points, so that the quartic-fit derivative is a reliable estimator of the quasiparticle-band slope at $k_F$ for the data sets and system sizes studied.

Given a discrete set of DMC-computed occupied-band energy values $\{\mathcal{E}(k_j)\}$ at reciprocal-space grid points $k_j$ available in a finite periodic simulation cell, the occupied-band shape on the interval $0\le k\le k_F$ is represented by fitting a quartic polynomial $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_0+\alpha_2 k^2+\alpha_4 k^4$ by least squares to the DMC points and using the analytic derivative $\partial\mathcal{E}/\partial k = 2\alpha_2 k + 4\alpha_4 k^3$ evaluated at $k=k_F$ to obtain the quasiparticle effective mass $m^{*}=k_F / (\partial\mathcal{E}/\partial k)_{k_F}$; this procedure assumes that a three-parameter quartic model is sufficiently flexible to capture the true band curvature over the fitting range so that the derivative at $k_F$ is reliable and not significantly biased by higher-order $k$-dependence or by fitting over points far from $k_F$.

For a single-particle energy band E(k) of a two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas, with scalar wave number k=|𝗸|, fitting discrete DMC-computed band values E(k_i) within the finite interval k∈[k_F−Δk,k_F+Δk] to the quartic polynomial E(k)=α_0+α_2 k^2+α_4 k^4 and then evaluating the derivative at k_F from the fit as (dE/dk)_{k_F}=2α_2 k_F+4α_4 k_F^3 provides an analytic approximation to the true thermodynamic-limit derivative at k_F suitable for estimating the quasiparticle effective mass m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F}, provided the chosen Δk is neither so small that pathological near-k_F fluctuations dominate nor so large that physics beyond quartic band curvature is sampled.

A practical protocol for extracting quasiparticle bands and effective masses in finite-size QMC calculations is: (i) compute the single-particle energy $\mathcal{E}(\mathbf{k})$ as the difference of many-body total energies obtained by adding or removing a single electron in a plane-wave orbital $\exp(i\mathbf{k}\!\cdot\!\mathbf{r})$ (instead of promoting an electron within the $N$-electron configuration), thereby avoiding extra contributions to the energy differences from interactions between an excited electron and the hole it leaves behind; and (ii) perform a least-squares fit of a smooth quartic function $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_0+\alpha_2 k^2+\alpha_4 k^4$ to the DMC band values over the entire occupied range and evaluate the derivative of that fitted function at $k_{F}$ to obtain the effective mass, which reduces sensitivity to Hartree-Fock-like residual pathological fluctuations and finite-size irregularities in the immediate vicinity of $k_{F}$.  Using single-determinant Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions that were optimized in the $N$-electron ground state and then used without reoptimization in the $(N\pm1)$-electron DMC calculations produces stable band values and effective masses that are insensitive to system-size trends that appear when only near-$k_{F}$ points are employed.

At each density, spin polarization, and finite system size the discrete DMC band values \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) (where \\(k=|\\mathbf{k}|\\)) were fitted over an extended wavevector interval to a quartic polynomial model
      $$
      \\mathcal{E}(k)=\\alpha_{0}+\\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\\alpha_{4}k^{4},
      $$
      with fitted coefficients \\(\\alpha_{0},\\alpha_{2},\\alpha_{4}\\). The quasiparticle effective mass was then obtained from the definition
      $$
      m^{*}=\\frac{k_{F}}{\\left.\\dfrac{d\\mathcal{E}}{dk}\\right|_{k_{F}}},
      $$
      where \\(k_{F}\\) is the Fermi wavevector of the infinite 2D HEG at the given density and spin polarization and the derivative is evaluated at \\(k=k_{F}\\). It was observed that extracting the derivative from fits restricted to a very narrow interval immediately surrounding \\(k_{F}\\) yields ill-behaved (pathological) estimates of \\(m^{*}\\), whereas performing the quartic fit over a larger modest wavevector range around \\(k_{F}\\) produces stable, systematically varying estimates of \\(m^{*}\\). Averaging the band behaviour over such a modest \\(k\\)-range therefore provides a controlled practical procedure to suppress the finite-cell and Hartree–Fock-like near-\\(k_{F}\\) fluctuations that cause the pathological behaviour.
